of Hybrid Search (combining BM25 keywords with 
Vectors) on these structural findings warrants further 
exploration. The shift to AI -mediated documentation 
consumption requires a shift in writing standards.

222 
Optimizing Technical Documentation for RAG Systems: A Comparative 
Analysis of Semantic Chunking vs. Structural Formatting 
Aashish Dhakal1, Shashank Shree Neupane1, Aadarsha Dhakal2, Muktinath Paudel1 
1Department of Information Technology, Presidential Graduate School, Kathmandu, Nepal 
2STARTsmall Pvt Ltd 
 
A R T I C L E I N F O  A B S T R A C T 
Article History: 
Accepted : 01 Jan 2026 
Published: 25 Jan 2026 
 
 As software documentation increasingly serves as the knowledge base for 
Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems, technical writers face a 
dilemma: should content be optimiz ed for human readability or machine 
re- trievability? This study investigates the impact of document structure 
(Standard Prose, Q&A Pairs, Fact -Dense Lists, and Structured JSON) and 
indexing strategies (Whole Document vs. Semantic Chunking) on vector 
retrieval performance. Using a controlled experiment with 250 retrieval 
trials across five technical domains (e.g., AWS Architecture, Django ORM) 
and the all -MiniLM-L6-v2 embedding model, we demonstrate a critical 
trade- off between precision and ranking stabil ity. Results indicate that 
Semantically Chunked Prose yields the highest precision (Top-1 Win Rate: 
28%), effectively ―sniping‖ specific queries. However, Question -Answer 
(Q&

What is the main objective of this docum.text:
A) for - matting offers superior ranking consistency (MRR: 0.54), 
acting as a retri eval safety net. Conversely, structured data formats like 
JSON-LD, despite their utility in traditional schema parsing, incur a 
significant penalty in vec - tor retrieval (Win Rate: 4%), revealing a 
―Semantic Gap‖ in current embedding architectures. We conc lude that 
technical documentation pipelines must evolve to prioritize natural 
language Q&A structures over purely syntactic schemas to maximize 
retrievability in RAG architectures. 
Keywords: Research Paper, Technical Documentation, RAG, Information 
Retrieval, Semantic Chunking, Vector Search, Knowledge Management.

INTRODUCTION 
The consumption of technical documentation is un - 
dergoing a fundamental paradigm shift. Histori- cally, 
developers navigated documentation via man- ual 
search: entering keywords into a search bar, scan- ning 
a list of blue links, and reading long -form arti- cles to 
extract relevant snippets.  Today, this work - flow is 
being rapidly replaced by AI-mediated inter- actions.

visibility. 
Focuses on the Generative phase 
(tone/authority), whereas we focus on the

A. Win Rate Analysis 
The ―Win Rate‖ represents the percentage of queries 
where a specific structure type appeared at Rank #1. 
 
TABLE 2 WIN RATE BY STRUCTURE TYPE (TOP-1 
ACCURACY) 
Structure Type Win Rate (%) 
Standard Prose (Chunked) 28.0% 
Standard Prose (Whole) 24.0% 
Q&A Format (Whole) 24.0% 
Fact-Dense (Whole) 20.0% 
Structured JSON (Whole) 4.0% 
 
As shown in Table 2, Semantically Chunked Prose  
achieved the highest Win Rate. This confirms the 
hypothesis that high -granularity indexing allows the  
retriever to ―snipe‖ specific queries. When a doc- 
ument is split into sentences, the vector for a specific 
sentence (e.g., ―Provisioned concurrency fixes cold 
starts‖) is mathematically much closer to the query 
vector than a vector representing a whole paragraph 
containing five different facts. 
B. Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR) Analysis 
MRR meas ures the overall ranking health.  A struc - 
ture that consistently appears at Rank 2 or 3 is often 
more valuable in production RAG systems than one 
that is either Rank 1

List the key points discussed in the doc.text:
International Journal of Scientific Research in Science, Engineering and Technology | www.ijsrset.com | Vol 13 | Issue 1 
Aashish Dhakal et al Int J Sci Res Sci Eng Technol, January-February-2026, 13 (1) : 222-227 
 
 
 
 
 224 
B. Experimental Design 
We selected five distinct technical domains. This 
diversity is crucial to e nsure our findings are not 
artifacts of a specific vocabulary (e.g., medical vs. 
computer science). 
1. AWS Cloud Architecture: (Topic: Cold Starts) 
2. Green Computing: (Topic: Power Usage Ef- 
fectiveness - PUE) 
3. Django Framework: (Topic: N+1 Query Prob- 
lem) 
4. Blockchain Technology: (Topic: Proof of 
Stake Consensus) 
5. AI Architecture: (Topic: RAG Components) 
C. Corpus Preparation and Chunking Strategy 
For each domain, we created five variations of the 
content. To ensure scientific validity, the semantic 
information (the core facts) remained identical across 
all variations; only the structure changed. 
For the ―Chunked‖ variations, we utilized a Recur- sive 
Character Text Splitter with the following hy- 
perparameters: 
• Chunk Size: 512 characters. 
• Chunk Overlap: 50 characters. 
• Separators: Paragraph breaks ‗\n\n‗ followed by 
sentence terminators.  
This configuration was chosen to approximate the 
standard retrieval window used in commercial RAG 
pipelines (e.g., LlamaIndex default settings). 
D. Variable Definitions 
1) Standard Prose (Whole). A continuous narra - 
tive paragraph typical of blog posts. Indexed as a 
sin- gle vector. 
2) Standard Prose (Chunked). The same narrative 
paragraph, but  split into atomic semantic units. 
This tests the hypothesis that smaller vectors are 
more pre- cise. 
3) Q&A Format. Explicit Question-Answer pairs 
(e.g., ―Q: What

metadata.author: TechnoScience Academy;www.ijsrset.com
metadata.title: International Journal of Scientific Research in Science, Engineering and Technology,IJSRSET
metadata.subject: Research Paper
metadata.creator: Microsoft® Word 2010

List the key points discussed in the doc.text:
Frequently Asked Questions section at the bottom of 
the page. This Q&A section acts as a ―Retrieval An- 
chor,‖ ensuring that if t he chunking strategy fails to 
capture the nuance, the explicit Q&A pair will catch 
the query vector. 
 
C. Implications for API Documentation 
Current API documentation (Swagger/OpenAPI) re- 
lies heavily on structured tables and JSON schemas. 
Our research sugges ts these formats are largely in - 
visible to RAG systems.  To make API docs ―AI - 
Ready,‖ engineers must generate natural language 
descriptions alongside the schema definitions. 
D. The Agentic Paradox: Retrieval vs. Action 
A nuance emerging from this study is the distinc- tion 
between RAG (Retrieval) and Agents (Action). While 
our data proves JSON is poor for retrieval, it remains 
the standard for function calling by Agents (e.g., 
OpenAI Functions). 
This creates a paradox: d ocumentation needs to be 
Prose to be found, but JSON to be used. We suggest a 
dual-layer approach for technical documentation 
sites: 1. Visible Layer: Natural language descriptions 
and Q&A for the Vector Index.  2. Hidden Layer: 
JSON schemas wrapped in HTML <details> tags or 
separate metadata fields, which are excluded from the 
embedding process but passed to the LLM context once 
the document is retrieved. 
 
CONCLUSION  AND FUTURE  WORK 
We have demonstrated that the structure of technical 
documentation significantly influences its retrievability 
in RAG systems. Contrary to traditional data modeling 
best practices, unstructured natural language 
(specifically Q&A pairs) outperforms structured data 
(JSON) in vector search tasks. 
Future work should investigate the role of Cross - 
Encoders (Re-ranking models). While this study 
focused on the bi -encoder retrieval step, it is possible 
that a powerful re -ranker could rescue the poor 
performance of JSON content if it reaches the 
candidate generation  stage.
